coredata.dc:title: Low-pressure thin-film composite nanofiltration membranes with enhanced selectivity and antifouling property for effective dye/salt separation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Juan
Gong, Ji-Lai
Fang, Si-Yuan
Cao, Wei-Cheng
Tang, Si-Qun

Wang, Yu-Wen
coredata.dc:description: For better sustainable resource recovery and elevating the separation efficiency of dye/salt mixture, it is essential to develop an appropriate nanofiltration membrane for the treatment of textile dyeing wastewater containing relatively smaller molecule dyes. In this work, a novel composite polyamide-polyester nanofiltration membrane was fabricated by tailoring amino functionalized quantum dots (NGQDs) and β-cyclodextrin (CD). An in-situ interfacial polymerization occurred between the synthesized NGQDs-CD and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on the modified multi-carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) substrate. The incorporation of NGQDs significantly elevated the rejection (increased by ∼ 45.08%) of the resultant membrane for small molecular dye (Methyl orange, MO) compared to the pristine CD membrane at low pressure (1.5 bar). The newly developed NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane exhibited enhanced water permeability without compromising the dye rejection compared to the pure NGQDs membrane. The improved performance of the membrane was primarily attributed to the synergistic effect of functionalized NGQDs and the special hollow-bowl structure of CD. The optimal NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane expressed pure water permeability of 12.35 L m-2h−1 bar−1 at the pressure of 1.5 bar. Noteworthily, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane not only showed high rejection for the larger molecular dye of Congo Red (CR, 99.50%) but also for the smaller molecular dye of MO (96.01%) and Brilliant Green (BG, 95.60%) with the permeability of 8.81, 11.40, and 6.37 L m-2h−1 bar−1, respectively at low pressure (1.5 bar). The rejection of inorganic salts by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was 17.20% for sodium chloride (NaCl), 14.30% for magnesium chloride (MgCl2), 24.63% for magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), and 54.58% for sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), respectively. The great rejection of dyes remained in the dye/salt binary mixed system (higher than 99% for BG and CR, <21% for NaCl). Importantly, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane exhibited favorable antifouling performance and potential good operation stability performance. Consequently, the fabricated NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane suggested a prospective application for the reuse of salts and water in textile wastewater treatment owing to the effective selective separation performance.

head.ce:title: Low-pressure thin-film composite nanofiltration membranes with enhanced selectivity and antifouling property for effective dye/salt separation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For better sustainable resource recovery and elevating the separation efficiency of dye/salt mixture, it is essential to develop an appropriate nanofiltration membrane for the treatment of textile dyeing wastewater containing relatively smaller molecule dyes. In this work, a novel composite polyamide-polyester nanofiltration membrane was fabricated by tailoring amino functionalized quantum dots (NGQDs) and β-cyclodextrin (CD). An in-situ interfacial polymerization occurred between the synthesized NGQDs-CD and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on the modified multi-carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) substrate. The incorporation of NGQDs significantly elevated the rejection (increased by ∼ 45.08%) of the resultant membrane for small molecular dye (Methyl orange, MO) compared to the pristine CD membrane at low pressure (1.5 bar). The newly developed NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane exhibited enhanced water permeability without compromising the dye rejection compared to the pure NGQDs membrane. The improved performance of the membrane was primarily attributed to the synergistic effect of functionalized NGQDs and the special hollow-bowl structure of CD. The optimal NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane expressed pure water permeability of 12.35 L m-2h−1 bar−1 at the pressure of 1.5 bar. Noteworthily, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane not only showed high rejection for the larger molecular dye of Congo Red (CR, 99.50%) but also for the smaller molecular dye of MO (96.01%) and Brilliant Green (BG, 95.60%) with the permeability of 8.81, 11.40, and 6.37 L m-2h−1 bar−1, respectively at low pressure (1.5 bar). The rejection of inorganic salts by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was 17.20% for sodium chloride (NaCl), 14.30% for magnesium chloride (MgCl2), 24.63% for magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), and 54.58% for sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), respectively. The great rejection of dyes remained in the dye/salt binary mixed system (higher than 99% for BG and CR, <21% for NaCl). Importantly, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane exhibited favorable antifouling performance and potential good operation stability performance. Consequently, the fabricated NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane suggested a prospective application for the reuse of salts and water in textile wastewater treatment owing to the effective selective separation performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Textile dyeing wastewater contains complex pollutants including organic dyes, inorganic salts, heavy metals, surface active dispersants, etc., causing serious harm to the environment and thereat public health [1–3]. A number of capable approaches have been established to treat the textile dyeing wastewater, such as ion exchange [4], electrocoagulation [5], photocatalysis [6], ozonation [7], adsorption [2], advanced oxidation [8], and membrane filtration [9]. Among these techniques, membrane filtration dominated by pressure-driven separation has been widely employed thanks to the advantages of modular design, easy operation, high efficiency, and convenient maintenance [9–11]. Especially, nanofiltration (NF) membranes had been implemented in the treatment of dyeing wastewater mainly dependent on the separation mechanisms of molecular sieving and charge effect [12–14]. It was beneficial to hierarchical targeted processing pollutants and conducive to resource recovery and cyclic utilization [14]. To acquire satisfactory separation efficiency, NF membranes with high rejection, good water permeability, and superior antifouling properties are eagerly needed.

In general, the commercial polyamide NF membrane was mainly fabricated by piperazine (PIP) or m-phenylenediamine (MPD) with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) via the interfacial polymerization (IP) process. The dense polyamide layer formed from PIP and TMC generally proved high rejection for both organic pollutants (200 – 1000 Da) and monovalent salts simultaneously, which was not quite suitable for selective separation treatment and recycling. One potential strategy to improve the separation efficiency is to prepare a selective layer with a relatively loose structure for NF membranes. β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) with a unique torus-shaped structure is a readily commercially available green natural material [15]. On account of its special intrinsic toroid structure containing plenty of hydrophilic hydroxyls, β-CD has attracted significant attention in improving membrane separation. The active hydroxyl groups in β-CD could react with TMC and the residual hydroxyl groups could regulate the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface [15]. Xiong et al. prepared a thin-film composite (TFC) membrane with ethylenediamine-modified β-CD (β-CD-EDA) and TMC [16]. The cavities of β-CD supplied extra water transport channels and the hydroxyl groups of β-CD enhanced the hydrophilicity, which resulted in the water permeability of the membrane being dramatically improved and obtaining excellent fouling resistance. Xue et al. fabricated TFC NF membrane using β-CD as the aqueous monomer and TMC as the organic monomer [17]. The resultant NF membrane not only presented high water permeability (98.8 L m-2h−1 bar−1) and dye rejection (95.6% for Congo Red and 94.4% for Methyl Blue) but also exhibited superior chlorine resistance [17]. It was attributed that the ester linkages formed by β-CD with TMC were not the attack sites by chlorine. However, the polyester β-CD NF membrane mainly aimed at the larger molecular dyes (such as Congo Red (molecular weight (Mw): 696.66) and Methyl Blue (Mw: 799.80)) [17,18]. In our previous work, the composite NF membrane was fabricated with the aqueous monomer of β-CD based on the multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [19]. It was found that the prepared β-CD NF membrane exhibited high rejection for Congo Red (96.39%) and Brilliant Green (97.41%) with high pure water permeability (179.93 L m-2h−1 bar−1). Whereas, the moderate rejection of resultant β-CD/TMC-MWCNTs membrane for relatively smaller dye molecules (Methyl Orange: 75.31%; Rhodamine B: 58.12%) [19]. Similar results were also collected from other literature [15,20]. In addition, the aggregation was easy to occur on account of the low solubility of β-CD in water, and thus the defects might be generated when the high concentration of β-CD [18]. Therefore, efforts are still needed to further improve the rejection of relatively small molecular dyes.

The structure and properties of the skin layer could be regulated by incorporating nanomaterials and then adjusting the performance of NF membrane. To further enhance the rejection of the membrane, one feasible approach is to introduce nanomaterials into the surface selective layer. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is carbon-based nanomaterial with zero-dimensional structure [21]. GQDs have the majority of properties of GO, including excellent compatibility, good chemical, and thermal stability, antibacterial property, as well as superior dispersion owing to hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in GQDs [21,22]. On account of these unique characteristics, GQDs has been gradually introduced into membranes to improve separation performance [23,24]. Zhang et al. fabricated a thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane with the incorporation GQDs into the tannic acid (TA) selective skin layer [25]. The obtained TFN membrane exhibited high dye rejection (Congo red: 99.8%; Methyl blue: 97.6%), enhanced water permeability (1.5 times more than that of the original TA TFC membrane), and better antifouling properties. It was mainly attributed to the improvement of TFN membrane surface characteristics with the introduction of GQDs. The more hydrophilic, smoother, and negatively charged surfaces endowed TFN membrane with better performance than TA TFC membrane [25]. Additionally, GQDs could be modified with various chemical groups or polymers, endowing them with different special properties and expanding their application prospects [26]. The addition of functionalized GQDs could regulate the diffusion of monomers and the cross-linking degree of the selective layer during IP process [26,27]. It was beneficial to the formation of a thin, defect-free, and smoother skin selective layer. The improved solute rejection, enhanced water permeability, better antifouling property and stability would be acquired by adding an appropriate content of functionalized GQDs, owing to the synergistic effects of the increased hydrophilicity, reduced filtration resistance, and extra water channels [27,28]. Comprehensively, functionalized GQDs is quite favorable for membrane separation and to be developed for greater application potential.

In this work, a kind of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGODs) were synthesized and were introduced into the β-CD aqueous monomer solution to prepare NGODs-CD embedded TFN membranes via in-situ IP process based on the MWCNTs substrate. The introduction of MWCNTs interlayer was not only used as the “bridge” to connect the active skin layer and the porous substrate, but also could regulate the properties of the active layer. The hydroxyl groups of β-CD and the hydrophilic functional groups in NGQDs (such as amine groups, hydroxyl groups, and carboxyl groups) could be beneficial to the mutual compatibility and the formation of defect-free active layer. The intrinsic hydrophobic cavity of β-CD would accelerate the transport of water. The addition of NGQDs could act as the extra “anchor” and enhance the connection between the active layer and the MWCNTs substrate. The conditions of membrane preparation were optimized. The effect of NGQDs concentration and the reaction time of IP process on the separation performance of the prepared membranes were investigated. The Methyl Orange (MO), Brilliant Green (BG), Congo Red (CR), Rhodamine B (RB), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), and magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) were chosen as the model dyes or salt pollutants, and the chemical structures of these dyes were shown in Fig. S1. The separation performances of the optimal composite membrane were assessed in single-component system (single dye or salt) and mixed-component system (salt and dye mixture) to explore the potential in separation of saline textile dyeing wastewater at low pressure (1.5 bar). In addition, the antifouling properties and operation stability performance of the composite NGQDs TFN membranes were also performed.

Polyvinylidene fluoride microporous membranes (PVDF, pore size: 0.45 μm) were purchased from Shanghai Xingya Purification Material Factory. Carboxyl functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, OD: 8 ∼ 15 nm, length: ∼ 50 μm, purity > 95%) were obtained from Chengdu Organic Chemicals Co. Ltd. Trimethylamine (TEA, AR, ≥ 99.0%), n-hexane (AR, ≥ 97.0%), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD, AR, ≥ 95.0%), citric acid (CA, AR, ≥ 99.5%), sodium alginate (SA, CP), Congo Red (CR, Mw = 696.66 Da, AR, ≥ 99.0%), Brilliant Green (BG, Mw = 482.63 Da, FMP/lnd), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, AR, ≥ 99.0%), sodium chloride (NaCl, AR, ≥ 99.5%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, AR, ≥ 98.0%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, ≥ 96.0%), and polyethylene glycol (PEG, CP, molecular weight (Mw) of 300, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 2000) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylene imine polymer (PEI, Mw = 70000, 30% aqueous solution) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Rhodamine B (RB, Mw = 479.01 Da, AR, ≥ 99.0%), Methyl Orange (MO, Mw = 327.33 Da, AR, ≥ 99.0%), and magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, AR, ≥ 99.0%) were supplied by Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Bovine serum albumin (BSA, BR, ≥ 98.0%) was provided by Hefei Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Humic acid (HA, CP) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. All chemicals and reagents mentioned in this study were used without further purification.

The synthesis of pristine GQDs was obtained by direct pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) as described in the literature [22]. Specifically, 2 g of CA was placed in the porcelain crucible and reacted in the oven at the temperature of 200 °C for 30 min. The state of CA was changed from white powder to orange liquation state and then the GQDs was synthesized during the process of incomplete carbonization of CA. After cooling down to room temperature, the GQDs solution was adjusted by NaOH (10 mM) solution to neutral. Subsequently, the orange liquid was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off 1000 Da) against ultrapure water for 3 days to remove residual CA. After that, the GQDs solution was freeze-dried to obtain solid GQDs.

The NGQDs material was prepared by solvothermal method with GQDs and PEI according to the reports with some modifications [29]. Firstly, the GQDs material (from 0.02 to 0.20 wt%) was dispersed into the PEI aqueous solutions (1.0 wt%) to produce homogeneous PEI-GQDs solutions. Secondly, the PEI-GQDs solution was transferred to the Teflon stainless steel reactor and reacted at 120 °C. The different solvothermal reaction time (from 10 h to 24 h) for PEI-GQDs solution were conducted. The obtained composite solution was denoted by NGQDs after cooling down.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of composite NGQDs NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The composite NGQDs NF membrane was prepared on the carboxylated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) substrate by the conventional IP method. Firstly, the MWCNTs modified support substrate was fabricated by vacuum-assisted filtration using uniform MWCNTs solution (0.01 wt%). Briefly, 5 mg of MWCNTs was dispersed into 50 mL of ultrapure water with ultrasound to acquire a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the MWCNTs solution after centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min) was vacuum-assisted filtered on the PVDF substrate. Then, the MWCNTs substrate membrane was fastened in the stainless-steel frame (home-made flanged blind plate device), and the aqueous solution was poured over and let sit for 20 min. The excess aqueous solution was drained off cautiously from the MWCNTs substrate using the rubbery roller. The aqueous solution consisted of NGQDs and 2.0 wt% β-CD. The 0.5 wt% TEA and 0.2 wt% NaOH was also added to the aqueous monomer solution as the acid-binding agent [19]. After that, the n-hexane solution of TMC was poured on the MWCNTs substrate impregnated with NGQDs and β-CD for 5 min. After pouring out the redundant TMC solution, the prepared membrane was washed with pure n-hexane for 30 s to remove the unreacted TMC. Finally, the prepared composite membranes were dried for 15 min at 60 °C. The obtained composite membrane was denoted as the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane.

Another method for preparation NGQDs composite membrane was used as vacuum assisted filtration approach. Firstly, the MWCNTs substrate was fixed on the vacuum filtration device. The aqueous monomer solution was poured in to cover the effective area of the MWCNTs support membrane for 20 min. After that, the aqueous solution was filtered. Then, the TMC n-hexane solution was poured onto the MWCNTs substrate immersed with aqueous solution. After the reaction was sustained for 5 min, the TMC n-hexane solution was poured out. The prepared membrane was taken out carefully and rinsed with pure n-hexane solution for 30 s. Finally, the prepared membranes were dried in an oven. A schematic representation of the preparation of composite NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was displayed in Fig. 1 . The formation of the polyamide-polyester thin layer occurred through the interfacial polymerization process. The amide bonds would form between the amine groups of NGQDs and the acyl chloride of TMC, and the ester bonds would form between the hydroxyl groups of β-CD and the acyl chloride of TMC. The possible schematic illustration of the formation of polyamide-polyester active layer was presented in Fig. 2 .

The control membranes were prepared by the flanged blind plate device with different aqueous monomers solutions (β-CD, PEI-CD, PEI, GQDs-CD, and NGQDs solution, respectively). The specific concentration of monomers and fabrication parameters were detailed in Table S1. The other preparation conditions were the same as the first method. The obtained membranes were denoted as CD membrane, PEI-CD membrane, PEI membrane, GQDs-CD membrane, and NGQDs membrane.

The surface morphology, cross-section structure, and thickness of the membranes were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300, Germany). The surface Zeta potential of the membranes was acquired by the Zeta potential analyzer for solid surface (SurPASS 3, Anton Paar, Austria). The atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension ICON) was used to examine the surface roughness of different membranes. The chemical structure and element composition of NGQDs and NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane were measured using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, America). The specifical chemical groups of the NGQDs materials, prepared freestanding NGQDs-CD membrane, and the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane were tested by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10). The molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was determined by filtration of different molecular weight of PEG (Mw = 300, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 2000). The MWCO value of the membrane was assessed by the molecular weight of PEG whose rejection reached 90% by the membrane [30]. The water contact angle of the membranes surface was carried out by Contact Angle Surface Analyzer (LAUDA Scientific GmbH，LSA-100, Germany).

The separation performance of the prepared membranes was evaluated by analyzing the rejection and permeability of small molecular dye (MO) by a home-made filtration device [31]. The prepared membranes were operated with pure water for at least 30 min at 1.5 bar to stabilize before measurement. The subsequent filtration pressure was maintained at 1.5 bar. The effects of the different preparation methods of the membranes, the contents of NGQDs, and the solvothermal reaction time of NGQDs were investigated on the performance of the membranes in this study. To determine the suitable IP reaction conditions, the reaction time of IP and the concentration of TMC were also conducted.

To evaluate the potential application in actual wastewater treatment, the optimal NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was selected to remove the single-component dyes with different molecular weights (RB, CR, BG, and MO) or salt ions (NaCl, Na2SO4, MgCl2, and MgSO4). Besides, the separation performance of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane for mixed-component system was further performed (using CR or BG as the model component for dye, and the NaCl or Na2SO4 for salt). The feed concentration of dyes and salts was 10 mg/L and 100 ppm, respectively. The concentration of dyes and salts was determined by UV–vis (Shimadzu, UV-2550, Japan) and the conductivity meter (Mettler Toledo S30 SevenEasy, Switzerland), respectively. The water permeability (P, L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) and the rejection (R, %) of the membranes were calculated according to the following formula (1) and (2) [32], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling properties and stability of the membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
Antifouling property is a crucial parameter for membrane application. To evaluate the antifouling properties of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane, the SA, BSA, and HA (1 g/L) were chosen as the model samples to conduct the filtration experiments [16]. In the antifouling experiment, the pure water and the model pollutant (SA, BSA, or HA) were alternately used as the feed solution for NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane. After measuring the pure water permeability (J0 , L m-2h−1 bar−1), the model pollutant (SA, BSA, or HA) was subsequently used as the feed solution to test the water permeability (JP , L m-2h−1 bar−1). Then, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was washed with pure water at least for 0.5 h. Afterwards, the water permeability (J1 , L m-2h−1 bar−1) was recorded with the pure water used as the feed solution. These three phases were regarded as one cycle period and two cycles (five phases) were performed in this experiment. The antifouling indexes of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane (including water permeability recovery rate (FRR), water permeability total decline rate (FDRt ), water permeability reversible decline rate (FDRr ), and water permeability irreversible decline rate (FDRir )) were calculated according to the following formulas (3)-(6) [33,34]:

The operation stability of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was conducted to estimate the potential of the membrane in practical application. The binary component system with CR/Na2SO4 or BG/Na2SO4 (10 ppm/100 ppm) mixtures were used as the feed solution. The water permeability and the rejection of dye and salts were measured every 1 h, and the whole filtration experiments were continued for at least 24 h. All the separation filtration experiments by NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane were performed at least three times and then the average value was used.

The surface morphologies of NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membranes, NGQDs membrane, and CD membrane were studied in detail via SEM and AFM, and the results were displayed in Fig. 3 and Fig. S3. The SEM images of the pure PVDF and MWCNTs supporting substrate were also investigated as a control (Fig. S2). The surface topography of the nascent PVDF substrate exhibited like “fan-shaped vein” structure. The surface and cross-sectional of PVDF substrate showed a loose and porous structure in nature (Fig. S2a, b). After modification with MWCNTs, the surface morphology of the MWCNTs substrate presented characteristic carbon tubes with mutually disordered staggered structures (Fig. S2c). The cross-sectional image manifested that the MWCNTs interlayer could be well-integrated with PVDF substrate (Fig. S2d), guaranteeing the potential mechanical stability.

In terms of three composite membranes based on MWCNTs substrate, it could be seen that the surface structures of the membranes formed by different aqueous monomers displayed different topographies (Fig. 3a, c, e). The SEM cross-sectional images of all membranes presented an asymmetric structure with a thin skin layer (Fig. 3b, d, f). The surface selective layer was well-combined with the MWCNTs interlayer. As shown in Fig. 3a, the surface of NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane exhibited a relatively smooth skin layer (Fig. S3a) with few “nanobubbles” [35]. The thickness of the surface skin layer was about 57.14 nm (Fig. 3b). The pure NGQDs membrane surface presented “ridge-like” structure with the thickness of ∼ 114.29 nm skin layer (Fig. 3c, d). Some tubular protuberances were observed on the surface of the CD membrane (Fig. 3e), which was similar to the surface morphology of the MWCNT support substrate. It was indicated that the formed skin layer of the CD membrane was ultra-thin. The cross-sectional of CD membrane was confirmed that the skin active layer was around 76.19 nm (Fig. 3f). The different thickness and morphology of the membranes were caused by a combination of several factors, such as the properties of aqueous monomer molecule and the diffusion rate, the uptake capacity of the interlayer, the release rate of the interlayer to the monomers, and the influence of nanobubbles during IP process [36,37]. The surface roughness average (Ra) of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane, NGQDs membrane, and CD membrane was about 47.6 nm, 63.0 nm, and 90.9 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). As a result, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane obtained significantly thinner and smoother membrane surface than the other two composite membranes. It might be attributed to the fact that NGQDs-CD compound monomer combined the long-chain structure of NGQDs and the special hollow-bowl-shaped 3D structure of CD, retarding the diffusion rate of NGQDs-CD monomer to the organic phase and forming the thinner selective layer than NGQDs membrane and CD membrane. Furthermore, the presence of the MWCNTs interlayer would prevent the incursion of skin layer [38], benefiting to the generation of thinner and smoother surface for the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane. Besides, the water contact angle of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membranes, NGQDs membrane, and CD membrane was 55.1°, 49.1°, and 45.5°, respectively (Fig. S4). It was indicated that the surfaces of these membranes were all hydrophilic.

To investigate the chemical structure of the NGQDs materials, freestanding NGQDs-CD membrane and NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane, FTIR analysis were performed and the results were showed in Fig. 4 . For the NGQDs materials, the peak at 3427 cm−1 was mainly due to the overlapping stretching vibration of –OH and NH [39]. The peaks at 1631 and 1576 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibration of CO and NH, respectively [27,40]. It was indicated that the amide groups were formed between the amine groups of PEI and the carboxyl groups of GQDs. In the spectra of freestanding NGQDs-CD, the peaks at 1724, 1298 and 1030 cm−1 were assigned to the CO and CO of ester groups, respectively (Fig. 4) [19,41]. It was generated from the reaction between the hydroxyl groups (–OH) in NGQDs-CD and the acyl chloride groups (-C(O)Cl) in TMC [15,41]. The peaks at 1621 and 1572 cm−1 were attributed to amide groups formed by reaction between the primary amine / secondary amine in NGQDs-CD with TMC [34]. It was confirmed that the structure of formed active layer was the coexistence of polyamide and polyester. In terms of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane, the characteristic peaks were also observed correspondingly with that of the freestanding NGQDs-CD membrane merely with some shift at location and change in peaks intensity (Fig. 4). It was verified that the polyamide-polyester active layer was successfully formed on the modified MWCNTs substrate membrane. The peaks at around 3374 and 3295 cm−1 were resulted from the stretching vibration of –OH and NH [26,39]. The CO and CO were shifted to 1708 and 1248 cm−1 respectively. The amide groups of CO and NH were observed at 1618 and 1562 cm−1, respectively. The shift of peaks and the changes of peak intensity for the composite NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane might be relevant to the connection between surface active layer and the modified MWCNTs substrate.

The chemical compositions of NGQDs and the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane were further investigated by XPS and the results were showed in Fig. S5 and Fig. 5 . The specific percentages content of species was showed in Table S2. The XPS survey of NGQDs materials could be observed C1s, N1s, O1s, and Na1s at 285.16 eV, 399.12 eV, 531.38 eV, and 1071.18 eV, respectively (Fig. S5). The presence of Na element in NGQDs might be resulted from the addition of NaOH during the adjustment of pH value. The C1s spectra of NGQDs could be divided into four peaks at 284.62 eV, 285.29 eV, 287.76 eV, and 288.54 eV, corresponding to CC / CC, CO / CN, N-CO, and O-CO, respectively (Fig. S5) [28,34]. The deconvolution spectra of N1 were observed peaks at 398.76 eV, 399.21 eV, 399.62 eV, and 401.14 eV, which could be assigned to –NH2, NO, HN-CO, and N-CO, respectively [28,39]. Furthermore, the peaks at 530.48 eV, 531.24 eV, 533.24 eV, and 535.29 eV might be attributed to the N-CO, O-CO, CO, and the absorbed water, respectively, in the spectra of O1s for NGQDs (Fig. S5) [34,42,43]. The XPS spectra analysis of NGQDs was demonstrated that the PEI was successful modified onto the GQDs materials via the formation of NO groups and amide groups. The XPS spectra of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane presented the similar results with the NGQDs (Fig. 5). The peaks at 284.59 eV, 285.12 eV, 286.41 eV, 287.79 eV, and 288.16 eV of C1s spectra for the membrane were assigned to CC / CC, CN / C-OH, CO, N-CO, and O-CO, respectively [27,34]. According to the Table S2, the percentage contents of O-CO groups in high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and CO groups in O1s spectra for NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane respectively were significantly increased. It was mainly attributed to the formed ester groups by the NGQDs-CD and TMC and the carboxylic groups hydrolyzed by the extra acyl chloride groups of TMC [19,34]. In addition, the unreacted hydroxyl groups in NGQDs-CD were also contributed to the increase of oxygen atomic contents. The N1s spectra of the membrane was divided into four peaks at 398.66 eV, 399.18 eV, 399.79 eV, and 401.17 eV, which were corresponding to –NH2, NO, HN-CO, and N-CO, respectively (Fig. 5) [28,39]. Compared to the NGQDs, the percentage content of NH2 was dramatically decreased while the HN-CO, and N-CO groups were increased for the N1s spectra of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane (Table S2). It was suggested that the IP reaction was occurred between the primary amine and secondary amine groups in NGQDs and the acyl chloride groups in TMC. It was consistent with the FTIR results.

The zeta potential of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane surface was showed in Fig. S6. The isoelectric point (IEP) of the membrane was estimated around 5.48 (Fig. S6). At low pH values (<5.48), the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane surface was electropositive. It might be due to the protonation of limited unreacted amino groups in the membrane. With the increase in pH, the protonation degree of amino groups decreased and the ionization degree of carboxyl groups gradually increased, resulting in the decrease of zeta potential for the membrane. The surface of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was negatively charged when the pH value over 5.48. The zeta potential of the membrane was about −61.43 mV when the pH value was around 7 (Fig. S6), which was favorable for the rejection of negatively charged pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimal selection experiments of the aqueous monomers
ce:section.ce:para: To select the appropriate aqueous monomers (CD, PEI-CD, PEI, GQDs-CD, NGQDs, NGQDs-CD) to prepare the composite membrane, the permeability and rejection of MO were performed and the results were displayed in Fig. 6 . These membranes were fabricated by the traditional IP process using a flanged blind plate device. As shown in Fig. 6a, the CD membrane had the largest pure water permeability (∼797.47 L m-2h−1 bar−1) while the rejection of MO was unsatisfactory. The rejection of MO by the CD membrane decreased from 97.92% to 19.40%, with the filtration volume increasing from 8 to 48 mL (Fig. 6b). It might attribute to the presence of a relatively large void between the CD and TMC [15]. The interconnection between CD and the MWCNTs interlayer was mainly hydrogen bonding, which caused the limited content of CD to be absorbed on the MWCNTs interlayer and might result in the presence of defects in the active layer of the CD membrane. Accordingly, the high permeability of pure water and the unsatisfactory rejection of MO by the CD membrane were obtained. The zeta potential of the CD membrane was −64.64 mV at pH ≈ 7 (Fig. S7). The electrostatic repulsion could contribute the rejection of negatively charged MO by the membranes. However, the relatively loose network structures between CD and TMC lead to less resistance for MO molecules through the membrane [15]. The adsorption between the MWCNTs interlayer of the membrane and the MO molecules was gradually saturation with the increasing filtration volume, resulting in the reduced rejection of MO by the membrane. Similar results occurred for the other membranes. The rejection of MO was decreased by 27.41%, 29.23%, 76.31%, 11.62%, and 14.31% for the PEI-CD membrane, PEI membrane, GQDs-CD membrane, NGQDs membrane, and NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane, respectively, with the filtration volume increased to 48 mL. The less reduction of the rejection of MO by the NGQDs membrane and NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was obtained with the increasing filtration volume. It was attributed to the special structure of NGQDs and conduced to form the denser network structures than other membranes (Fig. 2 and Fig. 3). The greater resistance generated by the denser selective layer contributed to high MO rejection while resulting in the relatively low water permeability (Fig. 6a).

The permeability of PEI-CD membrane was dramatically decreased (133.41 L m-2h−1 bar−1) and the average rejection of MO was improved to 85.66% compared to the pure CD membrane (Fig. 6). It was mainly ascribed to the reduction of void size within active layer with the addition of PEI to the CD aqueous monomer. Besides, the negatively charged surface of the PEI-CD membrane (-49.88 mV, pH ≈ 7) was conducive to the rejection of MO (Fig. S7). In comparison to the PEI membrane, the enhanced water permeability of the PEI-CD membrane was mainly due to the existence of CD with inherent internal cavity, which was provided extra transport channels for water [16]. As for the GQDs-CD membrane, the water permeability was declined and the rejection of MO was slight improved compared to the pure CD membrane. In terms of the NGQDs membrane, the average rejection of MO was increased to 94.51% while the water permeability was significantly decreased to 7.08 L m-2h−1 bar−1. With regards to the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane, both average rejection (95.82%) and water permeability (8.62 L m-2h−1 bar−1) were all slightly improved compared to the NGQDs membrane. It was mainly due to the thinner and defect-free active layer (Fig. 3) generating from the synergistic effect of NGQDs and CD monomer, endowing the better rejection of MO by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane than other membranes. Moreover, the extra water channels might be provided by the CD with intrinsic inner cavities [15], and thereby enhancing the water permeability of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane. In addition, the relatively high rejection rate of MO by the membrane was also beneficial from the strong electronegatively (-61.43 mV, pH ≈ 7). The zeta potentials of these membranes surface at pH ≈ 7 all displayed electronegativity (Fig. S7). However, the sequence of zeta potential of the membranes was not quite consistent with the order of the MO rejection by the membranes (Fig. S7, Fig. 6). It was indicated that the rejection of MO by these membranes was not only depended on the electrostatic interaction. The steric hindrance also contributed to the removal of MO, so that the different rejection and permeability of MO were acquired by the membranes with a nearly electronegative charge. Comprehensively considered, the NGQDs-CD aqueous monomer was chosen to prepare the composite membranes in further experiments.

The selective layer of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was obtained by the interfacial polymerization of aqueous monomers (NGQDs-CD) and organic monomer (TMC). The influences of the NGQDs content in the aqueous monomer solution on the performance of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane were displayed in Fig. 7 , where the CD and TMC concentration were fixed at 2.00 wt% and 0.20 wt%, respectively. The pure water permeability of the membrane was declined gradually from 10.48 to 8.39 L m-2h−1 bar−1 when the NGQDs content increased from 0.02 to 0.05 wt% (Fig. 7a). As the NGQDs content increased from 0.05 to 0.10 wt%, the pure water permeability of the membrane was slightly changed and the average value was 8.73 L m-2h−1 bar−1. However, the MO permeability was enhanced by about 47.88% with the content of NGQDs increasing from 0.05 to 0.10 wt%. It could be explained by the following reasons. The aqueous monomers solutions were dominated by the CD monomer at the low NGQDs concentration (0.05 wt%). The network structures of the skin selective layer of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane might be loose. The MO molecules could pass through the relatively loose top selective layer into the interior of the membrane and caused the blocking of the membrane pores. It resulted in the relatively low MO permeability of the membrane. As the content of NGQDs increased to 0.10 wt%, the pore size of the formed network structure would be further reduced. The skin selective layer presented comparatively complete and dense network structures. The MO molecules were rejected by the membrane instead of blocking in the membrane pores. It was contributed to the relatively high MO permeability compared to the low NGQDs concentration (<0.10 wt%). However, as the concentration of NGQDs continued increasing, more NGQDs monomers reacted with the TMC to form a denser selective layer based on the NGQDs-CD aqueous monomers. Thus, it led to a decrease in water permeability for the membrane when the NGQDs concentration was increased to 0.20 wt%. Correspondingly, the rejection of MO increased from 88.67% to 99.05% with the NGQDs content range of 0.02–0.20 wt% (Fig. 7b). These results indicated that the denser selective layer could be formed with the increasing NGQDs contents, contributing to the higher rejection rate and lesser water permeability. According to the results, the addition of 0.10 wt% NGQDs to the aqueous monomers containing 2.00 wt% CD could reach a better combined effect on the MO rejection and water permeability. Therefore, the NGQDs content was identified as 0.10 wt% to prepare the composite NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane with suitable separation performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of hydrothermal time of NGQDs and the operation method for preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal time of NGQDs might affect the connection between PEI and GQDs, and thereby influence the formation of active layer. The operation method for preparation membrane might have impact on the uptake of aqueous phase monomer on the MWCNT interlayer, which would make a difference on the construction of skin layer. The composite NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was fabricated by the flanged blind plate device and the vacuum assisted filtration method, respectively. The effect of hydrothermal time of NGQDs on the performance of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membranes prepared by two methods were showed in Fig. 8 . The rejection of MO by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane prepared by the flanged blind plate device was gradually decreased from 95.82% to 86.96% with the hydrothermal time increasing 10 h to 24 h (Fig. 8a). The pure water permeability of the membrane was first decreased and then increased with the hydrothermal time increasing. On the contrary, the rejection of MO by the membrane prepared by the vacuum assisted filtration method was first increased from 80.53% to 96.01% with the hydrothermal time varying from 10 h to 20 h, then slightly decreased to 94.69% when the hydrothermal time reached 24 h (Fig. 8b). It might be attributed to the degree of cross-linking between PEI and GQDs with hydrothermal time. The relatively more amino groups in NGQDs would be reacted with the organic phase monomer at the hydrothermal time of 10 h. With the hydrothermal time increasing, the more amino groups in PEI might connect with GQDs and the formed active layer by the cross-linked NGQDs chain might more tightly, causing the decline of water permeability. Whereas, with the hydrothermal time further prolonging, the less terminal amino groups in NGQDs could be reacted with TMC, causing the generation of relatively larger nanovoid within the active layer network and thus increasing the water permeability. Moreover, the aggregation might be occurred among the larger NGQDs molecular chain/cluster, resulting in the generation of defects on the skin layer and thereby leading to the decrease of rejection rate and the increase of water permeability. However, as for the vacuum assisted filtration method, the larger NGQDs molecular chain might be overlayed onto the MWCNTs interlayer with interlaced under external pressure driven. It might reduce the possibility of generating more defects in the subsequent IP process. Thus, the water permeability of the membrane was slightly decreased when the hydrothermal time was 24 h. In terms of the different change phenomena for the two preparation methods could be explained in the following respects. The NGQDs-CD aqueous phase monomer adsorbed on the MWCNTs interlayer was mainly depended on the initiative adsorption during the preparation of NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane using the flanged blind plate device method. However, the content of NGQDs-CD remaining on the MWCNTs interlayer might be reduced when using the vacuum assisted filtration method owing to the existence of the extra pressure. Additionally, the tighter connection between the NGQDs-CD and the MWCNTs interlayer might slow down the diffusion rate of NGQDs-CD, causing the thinner and uniform rejection layer for the later method. As a result, the water permeability of the membrane fabricated by the vacuum assisted filtration method was slightly higher than the flanged blind plate device method. In general, when the hydrothermal time was 20 h, the better rejection of MO was obtained (96.01%) by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane prepared by the vacuum assisted filtration method with the pure water permeability of 12.35 L m-2h−1 bar−1 (Fig. 8b). Therefore, the hydrothermal time of NGQDs was determined to be 20 h and the vacuum assisted filtration method was selected to preparation active layer during the interfacial polymerization process in the further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of TMC concentration and the reaction time of interfacial polymerization
ce:section.ce:para: The influences of the TMC concentration and the IP reaction time on the water permeability and MO rejection of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane were presented in Fig. 9 , where the NGQDs contents and the concentration of CD were kept constant at 0.10 and 2.00 wt%. The rejection of MO by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was increased from 76.48% to 97.34% while the pure water permeability was decreased from 34.69 L m-2h−1 bar−1 to 13.16 L m-2h−1 bar−1 with the reaction time of IP increasing from 0.5 min to 1 min (Fig. 9a). The rejection of MO was 95.97% ± 1.92% with slight fluctuation and the pure water permeability was 12.38 ± 1.00 L m-2h−1 bar−1 as the IP reaction time increased from 1 min to 5 min (Fig. 9a). When the IP reaction time was further increasing from 5 min to 10 min, the rejection of MO was slightly increased while the water permeability of the membrane was gradually declined. This phenomenon might be related to the thickness and properties of the active layer of the membrane varying with IP reaction time. The surface of the membrane displayed different morphological structure with the reaction time (Fig. S8) [44]. The formed active layer might be thin with defects due to the incomplete polymerization within the short reaction time (0.5 min), causing the low rejection of MO and high water permeability. In theory, the extent of polymerization was increased and the generated active layer gradually became thicker with the increase of IP reaction time [45], resulting in the decline of water permeability and the improvement of MO rejection (Fig. 9a). However, the relatively thicker of active layer (∼161.29 nm) was observed when the reaction time was 1 min (Fig. S8c). It might be attributed to the generated nanobubbles during the IP process (Fig. S8a), which led to the looser and rougher surface of the membrane. The presence of nanobubbles in the active layer might result in a rougher surface with extra extensive nanovoids and an increased effective filtration area, which was favorable for the enhancement of membrane performance [38]. As the reaction time was prolonged, some of the confined nanobubbles in the phase interface might be released off, thereby causing a relatively thin and denser active layer. A few nanobubbles and some aggregations on the surface of NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane were present when the reaction time was 5 min (Fig. S8d, e). The thickness of the membrane was around 58.06 nm (Fig. S8f). The thin active layer of the composite membrane endowed the relatively high pure water permeability (∼12.35 L m-2h−1 bar−1) with satisfactory MO rejection (∼96.01%). The water permeability of the membrane was gradually declined when the reaction time was over 5 min. It was attributed to the slow diffusion rate of NGQDs-CD, so that the membrane performance was merely slightly fluctuating during the reaction time from 2 to 5 min. When the IP reaction time was further increased to 10 min, the surface of the membrane was observed to have some obvious aggregations, and the thickness of the membrane was increased to about 151.61 nm (Fig. S8g-i). It resulted in the low water permeability of the membrane (Fig. 9a).

Fig. 9b showed the effect of TMC concentration on rejection of MO and water permeability by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane, where the IP reaction time was 1 min. With the TMC concentration increasing from 0.10 to 0.15 wt%, the pure water permeability of the membrane enhanced by ∼ 50% (Fig. 9b). The rejection of MO by the membrane was slightly increased. This variation might be explained by the following reasons. On the one hand, the diffusion of NGQDs-CD might be less resistance when the TMC concentration in organic phase monomer was relatively low (0.10 wt%), tending to form thicker active layer and thus resulting in low water permeability (Fig. 9b) [46]. On the other hand, the reaction of interfacial polymerization might be uncompleted at the low TMC concentration, leading to the formed active layers with larger network pores. The hydration caused by the abundant hydrophilic groups of NGQDs-CD might be the reason for the low water permeability [47]. Moreover, the high removal of MO might be resulted from the adsorption between amino groups in NGQDs-CD and the MO molecules. The complete cross network structure for active layer would be obtained with the increased TMC concentration. Meanwhile, the diffusion rate of NGQDs-CD would be retarded and induced to generate thinner active layer with the increased TMC concentration appropriately, benefiting to improve the water permeability and selectively. The rejection of MO by the membrane was 97.34% with the pure water permeability of 13.16 L m-2h−1 bar−1 as the TMC concentration reached to 0.20 wt% (Fig. 9b). However, the rejection of MO was slightly decreased with the TMC concentration further increasing from 0.20 to 0.30 wt% (Fig. 9b). It might be attributed to the swelling action [45]. Moreover, the higher TMC concentration might lead to a deficiency of NGQDs-CD during IP reaction and the excessive unreacted acyl chloride groups might reduce the degree of crosslinking of active layer, resulting in the decrease of MO rejection [46]. Therefore, the optimal TMC concentration was 0.20 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membranes
Separation properties and mechanisms of the membranes in single component system
ce:section.ce:para: Two anionic types of dyes (CR and MO), one type of cationic dye (BG) as well as one type of electroneutral dye (RB) were chosen to examine the separation performance of membranes. The nanofiltration performances for single different dye by NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-1 membrane and NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane were performed (the number represented the IP reaction time was 1 min or 5 min). As displayed in Fig. 10 a, the rejection of MO and CR by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-1 membrane was above 96%, the rejection of BG and RB was moderate. The permeability of the membrane for MO was 11.13 L m-2h−1 bar−1, which was higher than the permeability of other dyes. As the reaction time increased to 5 min, the high rejection of CR (99.50%) and MO (96.01%) was maintained by the membrane. The rejections of BG and RB by NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane were all increased (Fig. 10b). The order rejection of dyes by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was CR > MO > BG > RB. It might be attributed to the more complete structure for active layer owing to enhanced cross-linking degree derived from increased IP reaction time, as mentioned before. The permeability of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane only slightly fluctuated. The sequence of molecular weight of dyes was CR (696.66 Da) > BG (482.63 Da) > RB (479.01 Da) > MO (327.33 Da). The sequence of dye rejection by the membrane was not consistent with the order of molecular weight. It was indicated that the molecular size sieving was not the only mechanism for dye rejection. In addition, the MWCO of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was around 517 Da (Fig. S9). The small molecular of MO (Mw = 327.33 Da) obtained favorable rejection rate (96.01%) with a permeability of 11.40 L m-2h−1 bar−1 (Fig. 10b). It was mainly resulted from the electrostatic repulsion due to the negatively charged nature of MO and the electronegative membrane surface (pH ≈ 7, zeta potential: −61.43 mV; Fig. S6). The rejection of the positively charged BG might be caused by the presence of electrostatic attraction between dye molecules and the membrane surface. Therefore, the permeability of BG was lower than that of MO and CR due to the membrane fouling induced by adsorption (Fig. 10b). Meanwhile, the different rejection rates of dyes with the same charge (CR and MO) resulted from the molecular size sieving effect. The lightly higher rejection rate of CR than MO was mainly attributed to the fact that the molecular size of CR (2.63 nm × 0.73 nm) was larger than that of MO (1.49 nm × 0.48 nm) [15,48]. As for the zwitterionic dye of RB molecule, the rejection of RB by the membrane mainly resulted from the molecular size sieving effect. Although the RB molecule (479.01 Da) possessed almost the same molecular weight as the BG molecule (482.63 Da), the electrostatic interaction between RB molecules and the membrane was not obvious owing to the zwitterionic form in aqueous solution for RB [49]. Therefore, the rejection rate of RB (85.46%) was inferior to the BG (95.60%) by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane. It was worth mentioning that the rejection of CR, MO, and RB by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was increased by 3.11%, 20.70%, and 27.34%, respectively compared to the β-CD/TMC-MWCNTs membrane in our previous work [19]. It was demonstrated that the introduction of NGQDs into the selective layer indeed improved the rejection of smaller molecular dyes by the membrane.

The result of rejection for single inorganic salt by NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane presented in Fig. 11 a. The salt rejection by the membrane followed the order: Na2SO4 (54.58%) > MgSO4 (24.63%) > NaCl (17.20%) > MgCl2 (14.30%) (Fig. 11a). The rejection of salt by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was mainly attributed to the Donnan effect and size exclusion [50,51]. The surface of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was negatively charged under neutral conditions (pH ≈ 7, zeta potential: −61.43 mV, Fig. S6). According to the Donnan exclusion effect, the repulsive effect for divalent anion (SO4 2-) rejection was relatively strong than monovalent anion (Cl-) [33,51]. In order to maintain the balance of charges in the solution, the corresponding counter ions were also rejected. Moreover, the hydrated diameter of SO4 2- (0.76 nm) was larger than that of Cl- (0.66 nm) [40]. Therefore, the rejection of divalent anionic salts was superior to monovalent anionic salts with the synergistic effect of Donnan effect and size exclusion [33]. Although the hydrated diameter of Mg2+ (0.86 nm) was larger than that of Na+ (0.72 nm), the Donnan effect was dominant for the salt rejection [33,40]. Thus, the rejection of Na2SO4 was higher than the rejection of MgSO4 by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane with negatively charged. Owing to the hydrated diameters of salt ions (Na+: 0.72 nm; SO4 2-: 0.76 nm; Mg2+: 0.86 nm; Cl-: 0.66 nm) were close to the inner cavity size of CD (0.60 ∼ 0.78 nm) in NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane, resulting in the relatively low rejection for salts by the membrane [15,40,52]. It was also demonstrated that the Donnan exclusion effect was predominant for the different salt rejection by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane. The low rejection of salt was beneficial for selective separation by the membrane for dye/salt mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of the membrane in binary mixed component system
ce:section.ce:para: Separating dye/salt mixtures is significant for the purification and recovery of textile wastewater. In this study, BG and CR were chosen as the model dyes. NaCl and Na2SO4 were chosen as the model salts because both were commonly contained in practical textile wastewater. The water permeability and rejections of salt and dye by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane were illustrated in Fig. 11b. The water permeabilities of the membrane for dye/salt mixture were lower than those of single component solution. The rejection rates of dyes (BG and CR) by the membrane were all above 99% (Fig. 11b). The rejection rate of NaCl was slightly increased to 18.12% and 20.98% in CR/NaCl and BG/NaCl mixture solution, respectively (Fig. 11b). The rejection rate of Na2SO4 was increased by 36.27% and 16.14% in CR/Na2SO4 and BG/Na2SO4 mixture solution, respectively (Fig. 11b). The existence of salt ions promoted the rejection of dyes and salts by the membrane simultaneously. The improved rejection of dyes might be attributed to the aggregation of dye molecules due to the addition of salt [9,53]. The dye aggregation would occur owing to hydrophobic interactions between the aromatic rings of dye molecules [9]. The addition of salts would appropriately enhance the solubility of dyes in aqueous solution due to salt-in effect. The contact areas of dye molecules with the membrane surface were possibly increased due to the hydrophilic membrane surface and the better dispersion of dyes [53].The dye molecules would be accumulated on the membrane surface to form a dye cake layer or adsorbed on the membrane surface, generating another “charged surface”. It contributed to the rejection of dyes while declined the water permeability. In addition, the electrostatic shielding effect generated from the introduction of salt ions might affect the electrostatic repulsion between dyes and the membrane surface. Whereas, the screening effect might be weak due to the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane with strong negatively charged and the anionic dye of CR with relatively large molecular size (Fig. S6) [48]. Moreover, the concentration of salt was not high (100 ppm). Therefore, the high rejection rate of CR (>99%) by the membrane was kept in two dye/salt mixed component system (CR/NaCl, or CR/Na2SO4) thanks to the molecular size exclusion and electrostatic repulsion (Fig. 11b). For the positively charged BG, the slightly reduced electrostatic interaction might be beneficial to the rejection of the membrane for BG due to the characteristic of the oppositely charged for BG molecules and the membrane surface. Thus, the rejection of BG in dye/salt mixed component system (BG/NaCl, or BG/Na2SO4) by the membrane was slightly increased compared to the single dye component system. These results indicated that appropriate content of salt ions was favorable for the rejection of dyes by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane. Additionally, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane could separate effectively dye/monovalent salts mixed component system. As discussed above and shown in Fig. 12 , the separation mechanisms of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane for dyes were mainly electrostatic interaction and molecular sieving effect (Fig. 12). The hydrophobic effect between aromatic rings of dye molecules might also be taken into consideration [54]. The rejection of salt by the membrane was principally resulted from the Donnan effect [50,51]. The size exclusion also made a certain contribution to the rejection of salt by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane [33,50,55].

As shown in Table S3 and Fig. 13 , the separation performance of the prepared NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane for dyes with different molecular weight were compared to that of membranes related to β-CD or GQDs in some literatures. Many of reported membranes were obtained high rejection (92% ∼ 99%) for relatively larger molecule dyes (such as Methyl blue (Mw: 799.80 Da), CR (Mw: 696.66 Da), and Alcian blue (Mw: 1299 Da)) while for middle rejection for smaller molecule dyes (such as MO (Mw: 327.33 Da) and Orange GⅡ (Mw:452 Da)) [19,24,25,56]. Some researches could acquire favorable rejection for MO, but relatively high operation pressure was needed (4 ∼ 10 bar) [15,20]. In comparison, the prepared NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane exhibited superior dye rejection with comparable water permeability at low pressure (1.5 bar), including larger molecule dye of CR (Mw: 696.66 Da; 99.50%) and relatively smaller molecule dye of MO (Mw: 327.33 Da; 96.01%). It was suggested that the resultant membrane fabricated by the synergetic effect of NGQDs and β-CD in this work possessed relatively high separation performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling properties of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Fouling is an unavoidable problem during the operation process, shortening the lifetime of the membrane. Thus, a good antifouling property is a vital parameter for the membrane. The surface roughness and hydrophilicity of the membrane could influence the fouling behavior of the membrane. A better antifouling performance would be obtained with a lower surface roughness and higher hydrophilicity of the membrane [9]. The fouling behaviors of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane towards SA, BSA, and HA were estimated by comparing the water permeability before and after fouling by pollutants (SA, BSA, or HA) solution. It could be observed that the water permeabilities were all decreased when the pollutants (SA, BSA, or HA) solution was used as the feedstock (Fig. 14 a). It could result from the phenomenon of the concentration polarization and the effect of membrane fouling with the accumulation of pollutants with large molecular sizes [9]. The antifouling indexes of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane were displayed in Fig. 14b and the specifical values were represented in Table 1 . The water permeability recovery rate (FRR) of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was 86.23% for SA, 88.41% for BSA, and 96.83% for HA, respectively, in the first fouling cycle. At the same time, although the water permeability total decline rate (FDRt1 ) was 63.80% for SA, 35.32% for BSA, and 5.12% for HA, respectively, the water permeability irreversible decline rate (FDRir1 ) was only 13.77% for SA, 11.59% for BSA, and 1.96% for HA. It was suggested that the majority of pollutants were just in the form of physical attachment on the membrane surface and could be removed by simply washing with pure water, contributing to the significant recovery of the water permeability of the membrane [51]. At the second fouling cycle, the FRR2 of the membrane was maintained relatively at high values (97.20% for SA, 97.24% for BSA, and 99.11% for HA, respectively) and had a slight increase compared to the first cycle. The FDRir2 of the membrane was merely 2.80% for SA, 2.76% for BSA, and 0.89% for HA, respectively. In addition, the FRR value of the membrane toward HA was relatively higher than BSA and SA, indicating the better antifouling performance toward HA compared to BSA and SA. It might be attributed to the adsorption effect between SA and the membrane surface was comparatively stronger than BSA and HA, resulting in the removal not easily for SA in a short flushing time. Nevertheless, the results with high FRR and the low FDRir were indicated that the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane possessed favorable antifouling properties. It might benefit from the hydrophilic and smoother membrane surface (Fig. 3a). Besides, the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged pollutants and the electronegative membrane surface was also responsible for the satisfactory antifouling performance [33,51].

Moreover, the FRR of some membranes in literatures were summarized in Table 2 . It was worth noting that the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane presented superior water permeability recovery rate toward BSA, HA, and SA in comparison with other reported literature [9,26,57–60]. Particularly, the FRR of NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was increased by ∼ 52.1% compared to the β-CD/TMC-MWCNTs membrane (∼45.1%) towards BSA [19]. It was suggested that the resistance to protein contamination for β-CD membranes was significantly elevated by the incorporation of NGQDs. It was confirmed that the deposited pollutants on the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane surface could be readily washed away to recover the separation performance to a comparatively high level owing to the smoother membrane surface and negatively charged [51] (Fig. 3). Furthermore, the FDRir of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was low. Thus, it was indicated that the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane with high FRR and low FDRir possessed a better antifouling property.

The operation stability of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was tested continuously for 24 h and the filtration performance was exhibited in Fig. 15 and Fig. S10. The average rejection of CR by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane remained at 99.30%, and the rejection of BG was only slightly decreased (about 12.57%) during 24 h operation time (Fig. 15). It was indicated that the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane possessed good rejection performance for the dyes during 24 h operation. The permeability of the membrane had a gradually decreasing trend in CR/Na2SO4 or BG/Na2SO4 solution. Specifically, at the first 8 h operation time, the permeability of the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane was gradually decreased from 4.56 to 2.16 L m-2h−1 bar−1 in the CR/Na2SO4 solution and from 5.35 to 2.32 L m-2h−1 bar−1 in the BG/Na2SO4 solution, respectively. The decrease of permeability of the membrane might be attributed to the concentration polarization and membrane fouling [9,63]. The water flushing was performed toward the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane after 8 h of the filtration experiment. It could be observed that the permeability of the membrane was recovered about 77% for the CR/Na2SO4 solution, and about 64% for the BG/ Na2SO4 solution, respectively (Fig. 15). It was indicated that the used NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane could be simply washed with pure water and to recover the most of membrane performance. The rejection of Na2SO4 by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane decreased slowly from 98.98% to 41.66% in the CR/Na2SO4 solution during the experiment (Fig. S10). In BG/Na2SO4 solution, the rejection of Na2SO4 decreased from 92.40% to 18.15% at the first 8 h (Fig. S10). Nevertheless, the rejection of Na2SO4 was increased to 85.72% after the membrane washing with pure water for at least 30 min. After that, the rejection of Na2SO4 gradually decreased to 9.59% (Fig. S10). The decreased rejection of Na2SO4 might be attributed to the decrease of charge intensity of the membrane surface owing to the occurrence of electrostatic attraction between the positive charged BG and the negatively charged membrane surface. The less reduction of rejection for Na2SO4 was obtained in CR/Na2SO4 solution compared to the Na2SO4 rejection in BG/Na2SO4 solution by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane. This phenomenon might be due to the presence of electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged CR and the negatively charged membrane surface (Fig. S6), resulting in less fouling effects onto the membrane surface and considerably keeping the rejection ability of the membrane for solute (Na2SO4 or CR).

In this work, the synthesized NGQDs and β-cyclodextrin (CD) were used as the aqueous phase monomer to react with the TMC for fabrication of a novel composite polyamide-polyester nanofiltration membrane via in-situ interfacial polymerization method on the MWCNTs substrate. With the introduction of NGQDs, the average rejection of smaller molecule dye of MO by the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs membrane was improved by 45.08% compared to the CD membrane at low pressure (1.5 bar). The optimal NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane with thin active layer (∼57.14 nm) presented relatively high pure water permeability (12.35 L m-2h−1 bar−1). The optimal NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane presented superior rejection not only for relatively larger molecule dyes (CR, Mw: 696.66 Da; 99.50%) but also for smaller molecule dyes (MO, Mw: 327.33 Da; 96.01%), while other researches merely obtained moderate rejection for smaller molecule dyes [24,25,56]. In comparison, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane exhibited excellent dye rejection with comparable water permeability at low pressure (1.5 bar). The rejection mechanisms of the membrane for different dyes were mainly the synergistic effect of molecular sieving effect and electrostatic interactions. Additionally, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane exhibited excellent dye selective separation performance (99.44% for CR, and 99.86% for BG) in dye/NaCl binary mixture component system. Moreover, the NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane presented superior fouling resistance to HA, SA, and BSA with a high FRR of nearly 99.11%, 97.20%, and 97.24%, respectively, which was better than other reported literature [26,59,60]. Therefore, the prepared NGQDs-CD-MWCNTs-5 membrane had a prospect of application in textile wastewater treatment to concentrate dyes and recover salts due to its high selective separation of dye/salt and better antifouling properties. The further work could be concentrated on exploring the applications in different fields towards removing different kinds of pollutants. In addition, further efforts need to be made to fabricate specific selective functional nanofiltration membranes that can remove target contaminants.
